denial_reason: Not medically necessary per clinical guidelines

timeline.event: Assessing whether all required information is present
All required information confirmed — beginning denial analysis
Analysis complete — win probability 65%, urgency: high

analysis.summary: Aetna denied Jane Doe's lumbar spine MRI (claim CLM-2025-99999, member AET-555123456) citing lack of medical necessity per clinical guidelines.
analysis.strategy: Challenge the denial by obtaining a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from the treating provider documenting failed conservative treatments, functional limitations, and specific clinical indicators that meet or exceed Aetna's own clinical policy bulletins for lumbar MRI. Request a copy of the specific clinical guideline Aetna applied and challenge any deviation from the ordering physician's clinical judgment. If internal appeal fails, escalate to an Independent Medical Review given that lumbar MRI denials based on medical necessity are frequently overturned when adequate clinical documentation is presented.
keyArgs: Aetna's denial fails to account for the treating physician's direct clinical evaluation and professional judgment, which carries significant weight under established medical necessity standards

Lumbar spine MRI is consistent with Aetna's own Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria when conservative treatment of 4-6 weeks has been attempted and failed, or when red flag symptoms are present — the denial letter does not specify which criteria were unmet

A blanket 'not medically necessary' denial without identifying the specific guideline provision or clinical finding that was deficient violates the requirement for a clear and specific denial rationale under ERISA Section 503

Denial of diagnostic imaging that prevents accurate diagnosis may constitute a denial of covered benefits, as treatment cannot be appropriately tailored without imaging to rule out serious pathology such as disc herniation, stenosis, or malignancy

Under ERISA's full and fair review requirement, Aetna must provide all clinical criteria used in the denial decision and allow the claimant to respond to any new evidence or rationale introduced at the appeal stage
nextSteps: Immediately contact Aetna at the member services number on your insurance card to request the complete denial letter, the specific clinical guideline or policy bulletin applied, and the credentials of the reviewing clinician

Obtain a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from the ordering provider documenting diagnosis codes, symptom duration, functional impairment, all prior conservative treatments attempted and their outcomes, and specific clinical reasons why MRI is required

Compile supporting clinical records including office visit notes, physical therapy records, prior imaging, medication history, and any documented red flag symptoms (e.g., neurological deficits, bowel/bladder dysfunction, unexplained weight loss)

File a formal Level 1 internal appeal in writing to Aetna referencing claim CLM-2025-99999 and member ID AET-555123456, attaching all clinical documentation and a point-by-point rebuttal of the medical necessity denial

If the internal appeal is denied or not resolved within 30 days, file for an Independent Medical Review (IMR) or External Review through your state insurance commissioner or under ERISA, depending on plan type
Document all communications with Aetna including dates, representative names, and call reference numbers to preserve a record for potential escalation or legal action
escalationPath: Level 1 Internal Appeal to Aetna's Appeals and Grievance Unit with full clinical documentation package
External Independent Medical Review through the applicable state Department of Insurance or federal external review process under the ACA/ERISA
File a complaint with the state Department of Insurance and/or consult an ERISA or insurance bad faith attorney if external review is unfavorable or if Aetna fails to follow procedural requirements
legalCitations: ERISA Section 503 (29 U.S.C. § 1133) — requires plan to provide specific reasons for denial and a full and fair review process
29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 — DOL claims procedure regulations requiring disclosure of all clinical criteria used and opportunity to respond
ACA Section 2719 (42 U.S.C. § 300gg-19) — mandates internal appeals and external review rights for health plan denials
Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Bruch, 489 U.S. 101 (1989) — establishes de novo review standard for ERISA benefit denials where plan does not grant discretionary authority
evidenceNeeded: Detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from the ordering physician specifying diagnosis, symptom duration, failed conservative treatments, and clinical rationale for MRI
Complete clinical notes documenting the patient's history of lumbar complaints, physical examination findings, and any neurological symptoms
Records of prior conservative treatment attempts (physical therapy, medications, chiropractic care) with dates and outcomes demonstrating failure to resolve symptoms
Copy of Aetna's applicable Clinical Policy Bulletin for lumbar spine MRI to identify and directly rebut the specific criteria cited as unmet
Any relevant prior authorizations, referral records, or specialist notes that corroborate the medical necessity of advanced imaging
